Metals Comparative Signal
← Back to dashboard

Metals Comparative Signal

Cross-metal relative valuation and z-score dashboard for precious and base metals.

As of 2026-03-16

Gemini Summary

Signal Summary:

  • Configuration statement: Given Silver (Z: 5.214), Gold (Z: 4.668), and Copper (Z: 2.402) remain in "Overbought" territory, this setup aligns with Downward-biased price paths and Elevated volatility, where the dominant risk is Mean reversion, not Trend continuation.
  • The metals complex is in a synchronized Extreme Overbought regime, with Zinc serving as the sole bullish outlier.
  • Conviction Band: Extreme; Interpretation Confidence: High Confidence; Internal Conflict Flag: No. Signal Stability Assessment: Deteriorating; Threshold Proximity: Far; Revision Sensitivity: Low.

Methodology Applied:

  • Z-scores exceeding 2.0 trigger a "Strong Sell" classification, identifying overbought conditions (source table).
  • Values between 1.0 and 2.0 indicate "Above average" pricing, triggering a "Sell" signal (source table).
  • Negative Z-scores below -0.75 trigger a "Buy" signal based on "Below average" relative valuation (source table).
  • Latest comparative observation: 2026-03-16.

Key Dynamics:

  • Silver (1) and Gold (2) drive the signal with extreme Z-scores (>4.5), indicating severe price-to-mean stretching.
  • Copper (3), Platinum, and Aluminium are clustered in the "Strong Sell" zone, confirming broad-based metals overvaluation.
  • Gold-Oil ratios have reached the 96th percentile, highlighting precious metal expensive-ness relative to energy inputs.
  • Conditional Invalidation: Broad Z-score normalization (reversion to <1.0) without a corresponding price decline.
  • Stability: Signal is deteriorating as prices have begun retreating from early-March peaks.

Scenario Balance:

  • Base case dominant: Mean reversion lower across precious and base metals as overbought extremes exhaust.
  • Most plausible upside risk: Geopolitical or liquidity shocks driving a "melt-up" in safe-haven precious metals.
  • Most plausible downside risk: Liquidation of crowded long positions leads to a synchronized, high-volatility correction.

Time Horizon & Aggregation:

  • Tactical (weeks); Z-score extremes of this magnitude typically resolve through short-term price adjustments.
  • Aggregation Weight Hint: High; extreme statistical deviations usually dominate macro sentiment and positioning flows (4).

Macro Relevance:

  • Informs Pricing and Sentiment dimensions of the global macro cycle.
  • Economic mechanism: Extreme price stretching implies speculative exhaustion and potential "blow-off top" dynamics.
  • Cycle position: Not determined.
  • Typically interacts with USD strength and real yield signals to determine the catalyst for reversal.

Regime Context:

  • Persistent Overbought regime (duration > 3 weeks based on history).
  • Direction of change: Weakening (prices currently rolling over from record highs).

Model Limitations:

  • Z-scores capture historical relative stretching but do not account for fundamental regime shifts in demand.
  • Zinc and Palladium divergence suggests idiosyncratic supply/demand factors may offset broad sector trends.

Data & References:

  • Metals Comparative Dashboard, 2026-03-16.
  • Silver and Gold Z-scores are the most influential drivers of the current "Strong Sell" cluster.
  • Public datasets on global exchange inventory levels would improve supply-side reliability.
